threats.title: AgentDAO Vacuous Success: Proposals Auto-Succeed When Token TotalSupply is 0 at Snapshot
threats.hypothesis_description: The AgentDAO.state() override returns ProposalState.Succeeded when forVotes == getPastTotalSupply(snapshot). If the proposal is created in the same block as the DAO/token deployment, the snapshot (clock()-1 = previous block) precedes token minting, making getPastTotalSupply return 0. Since forVotes is also 0, the condition 0==0 evaluates true, making ANY Active proposal immediately Succeeded. Combined with the ContributionNft admin's ability to bypass the proposal threshold, this allows arbitrary proposal execution on newly created AgentDAOs.
threats.investigation_rationale: The 0==0 edge case is a classic vacuous truth bug in governance contracts. The code has no guard against zero totalSupply, and the ContributionNft admin can create proposals bypassing threshold checks, making exploitation straightforward.

proof_of_signal.pattern: forVotes == token().getPastTotalSupply(proposalSnapshot(proposalId)) with no zero-check; 0==0 returns Succeeded

preconditions: AgentDAO is newly created with token supply 0 at snapshot block
ContributionNft admin creates proposal in same block or before token minting
votingDelay is 0 (set in initialize)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: When both are 0, any Active proposal becomes Succeeded; execute() is callable immediately
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No check that getPastTotalSupply > 0 or forVotes > 0

analysis_questions: What is the exact block delay between DAO creation and token minting in AgentFactoryV3?
Can the ContributionNft admin reliably create a proposal before token supply exists at the snapshot?
What assets are accessible through executed proposals on a new AgentDAO?
validation_requirements: Deploy AgentDAO and token, create proposal in same block, verify state() returns Succeeded
Verify execute() succeeds with arbitrary targets when forVotes==totalSupply==0
Test with ContributionNft admin bypassing threshold

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker who is ContributionNft admin creates a proposal targeting the DAO's TBA in the same block as agent creation. Snapshot at block-1 shows 0 supply. state() returns Succeeded. execute() drains TBA assets.
prerequisites: ContributionNft admin access or 0 proposal threshold
Proposal created before token supply exists at snapshot block

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete drain of AgentDAO TBA assets for newly created agents
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All token holders of the affected agent

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for 0-totalSupply edge case in state() override

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: None - just needs ContributionNft admin access
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring proposal creation on new DAOs

threats.title: Genesis.onGenesisSuccess Repeatable: Operator Can Overwrite User Token Claims
threats.hypothesis_description: onGenesisSuccess has no terminal state flag after successful execution. The only guard is refundUserCountForFailed==0, which remains true after a success call. The claimableAgentTokens mapping uses assignment (=) not addition (+=), meaning a second call overwrites all previous unclaimed allocations. OPERATION_ROLE can redirect all unclaimed agent tokens to attacker-controlled addresses.
threats.investigation_rationale: The absence of an isSucceeded flag and the use of = instead of += for claimableAgentTokens creates a concrete re-entrancy in the state machine. A backend bug or compromised OPERATION_ROLE key could trigger a second call.

proof_of_signal.pattern: claimableAgentTokens[addr] = amount (assignment, not +=); no isSucceeded flag check

preconditions: OPERATION_ROLE calls onGenesisSuccess successfully once
Some users have not yet called claimAgentToken
OPERATION_ROLE calls onGenesisSuccess again with different distribution

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No state flag preventing repeated onGenesisSuccess; claimableAgentTokens uses = not +=
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Second call overwrites unclaimed allocations, redirecting tokens
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No isSucceeded boolean or equivalent terminal state guard

analysis_questions: Can onGenesisSuccess be called twice with the same genesis after agentTokenAddress is set?
Does the second call correctly pass all modifier checks?
What happens to partially claimed vs unclaimed tokens?
validation_requirements: Call onGenesisSuccess twice, verify second call overwrites first allocations
Verify first allocation users lose their claimable tokens
Check if any modifier prevents the second call

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: After legitimate distribution, OPERATION_ROLE calls onGenesisSuccess again with different distributeAgentTokenUserAddresses pointing to attacker addresses. Original users' claimableAgentTokens are zeroed out.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Total loss of all unclaimed agent tokens from genesis
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All genesis participants who haven't claimed

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for repeated onGenesisSuccess calls

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Any time after first successful call
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring duplicate onGenesisSuccess events

threats.title: AgentTax.dcaSell Uses Incorrect Slippage Calculation Based on Input Amount
threats.hypothesis_description: dcaSell calculates minOutput as (amountToSwap * (DENOM - slippage)) / DENOM, treating the input taxToken amount as if it equals the expected assetToken output. If taxToken and assetToken have vastly different values, this calculation is meaningless: either minOutput is far too high (causing permanent DoS) or far too low (providing zero slippage protection). The correct calculation should use router.getAmountsOut to determine expected output first.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a mathematical error in the slippage formula. _swapForAsset (called by handleAgentTaxes) correctly uses router.getAmountsOut for its minOutput, but dcaSell bypasses this pattern with the incorrect input-based calculation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: minOutput = ((amountToSwap * (DENOM - slippage)) / DENOM); uses input token amount as basis for output token slippage

preconditions: EXECUTOR_ROLE calls dcaSell
taxToken and assetToken have different exchange rates (i.e., not 1:1)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: If taxToken is worth much less than assetToken, minOutput is too high and all swaps revert (DoS). If worth more, minOutput provides no protection.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should call router.getAmountsOut first, then apply slippage to the expected output

analysis_questions: What is the actual exchange rate between taxToken and assetToken in production?
Does dcaSell always revert or always provide zero protection?
How much tax revenue is at risk?
validation_requirements: Calculate minOutput for realistic exchange rates and compare to expected output
Test dcaSell with production token pair to verify it either reverts or allows sandwich
Compare dcaSell slippage calculation to handleAgentTaxes's correct approach

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If taxToken trades at 0.001 assetToken, dcaSell sets minOutput to ~0.999*amountToSwap which is 999x the expected output. All dcaSell calls revert permanently, locking accumulated tax revenue. Alternatively, if taxToken is more valuable, minOutput approaches 0 relative to actual output.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Either permanent DoS on tax distribution or complete value extraction via inadequate slippage
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: handleAgentTaxes can still be used as workaround

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test comparing dcaSell minOutput to actual expected output

threats.title: Deliberate Vote Type Enables Double-Voting in AgentDAO and VirtualGenesisDAO
threats.hypothesis_description: In GovernorCountingSimpleUpgradeable._countVote, voting with support=3 (Deliberate) returns early WITHOUT setting hasVoted[account]=true. This allows the same account to call castVote again with a real vote type (For/Against/Abstain), effectively getting two VoteCast events and the ability to strategically delay their actual vote after observing others. While the on-chain vote weight is only counted once, the emitted events mislead off-chain governance systems.
threats.investigation_rationale: The Deliberate type is intended as a signal-only vote, but by not setting hasVoted, it creates a loophole where a voter can participate twice in the voting process. In AgentDAO specifically, this could affect score tracking if the interaction with _castVote override is not properly handled.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (support == uint8(VoteType.Deliberate)) { return; } — returns without setting hasVoted[account] = true

preconditions: Voter has voting weight on an Active proposal
Voter first calls castVote with support=3 (Deliberate)
Voter then calls castVote again with support=0/1/2

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Early return for Deliberate vote without setting hasVoted flag
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Voter can cast a non-binding Deliberate vote, observe other votes, then cast real vote — breaking one-person-one-vote-timing invariant
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Deliberate should either set hasVoted=true or use separate tracking

analysis_questions: Does the double VoteCast event affect off-chain governance dashboards?
In AgentDAO._castVote, is the score correctly handled when Deliberate precedes a real vote?
Does the strategic voting advantage matter on an L2 with no public mempool?
validation_requirements: Cast Deliberate vote, then cast For vote, verify both succeed
Check AgentDAO score increment behavior across the two votes
Verify VoteCast events emitted for both calls

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Voter with large voting power signals Deliberate to appear neutral, observes other votes on-chain (after they're mined), then casts their actual vote strategically. Two VoteCast events confuse governance aggregators.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Strategic governance manipulation; misleading event emissions
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All DAO participants relying on accurate vote tracking
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: N/A — governance decisions may already be affected

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring duplicate VoteCast events per account

threats.title: Genesis.participate Per-Transaction Cap Not Per-User: Whales Can Dominate Allocation
threats.hypothesis_description: Genesis.participate checks virtualsAmt <= maxContributionVirtualAmount per transaction but does NOT check the cumulative mapAddrToVirtuals[msg.sender]. A user can call participate() multiple times with amounts up to maxContributionVirtualAmount each, accumulating far more than the intended cap. This defeats the purpose of limiting individual contributions.
threats.investigation_rationale: The variable name 'maxContributionVirtualAmount' strongly implies a per-user cap, but the implementation only enforces a per-transaction limit. The cumulative mapping is incremented with += but never checked against the cap.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(virtualsAmt <= maxContributionVirtualAmount) checks single tx, not cumulative mapAddrToVirtuals[msg.sender] + virtualsAmt

preconditions: Genesis is in Active state
User has sufficient VIRTUAL tokens and allowance

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Per-tx cap instead of per-user cumulative cap
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: mapAddrToVirtuals[msg.sender] += virtualsAmt accumulates without limit check

analysis_questions: Is maxContributionVirtualAmount intended as a per-user or per-transaction limit?
How does off-chain distribution handle users who over-contributed?
Can a whale receive disproportionate agent tokens?
validation_requirements: Call participate() twice with maxContributionVirtualAmount, verify total exceeds cap
Check if off-chain distribution normalizes or if on-chain state determines allocation

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Whale calls participate(1, maxContributionVirtualAmount) 100 times, contributing 100x the intended maximum. In distribution, they receive a disproportionate share of agent tokens.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All other genesis participants receive diluted allocation
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Off-chain distribution could correct, but on-chain state allows over-contribution

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for cumulative contribution exceeding cap

threats.title: AgentDAO._tryAutoExecute Causes Voter DoS When Proposal Target Reverts
threats.hypothesis_description: _tryAutoExecute is called inside _castVote when support==1 (For). If forVotes reaches totalSupply, execute(proposalId) is called inline during the voter's castVote transaction. If any proposal target reverts, the execute call reverts, which reverts the entire castVote transaction. The final For voter can never successfully vote, permanently blocking unanimous auto-execution.
threats.investigation_rationale: The auto-execute happens inside the voting path. A malicious proposal can intentionally target a reverting contract. Since the revert propagates up through castVote, the vote itself is rejected. This prevents anyone from being the 'last For voter'.

proof_of_signal.pattern: execute(proposalId) called inside _castVote; revert in execution propagates to voter's transaction

preconditions: Proposal targets a contract that will revert on execution
All but one token holder have voted For
Last holder tries to vote For, triggering auto-execute

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: execute() called inline during castVote; no try/catch wrapper
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Reverting target causes voter DoS; no voter can achieve unanimous for-vote
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should wrap execute() in try/catch or separate auto-execution from voting

analysis_questions: Can proposals still succeed via normal execution after voting period ends?
What is the gas cost impact of inline execution during voting?
Can this be used to grief all auto-execution on an AgentDAO?
validation_requirements: Create proposal targeting a reverting contract
Have all holders vote For except one
Verify last For vote always reverts

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker creates a proposal targeting a contract that always reverts (e.g., self-destructed or intentionally reverting receive). When voting reaches n-1 For votes, the last voter's For vote triggers _tryAutoExecute which calls execute, which reverts the entire castVote.
prerequisites: Proposal threshold met or admin bypass

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: DoS on unanimous auto-execution feature; proposals stuck in Active until timeout
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All voters on the affected proposal
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Proposal can still execute after voting period if quorum/majority met

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for auto-execute when proposal target reverts

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring failed castVote transactions

threats.title: Airdrop Contract Missing Array Length Validation and Total Reconciliation
threats.hypothesis_description: The Airdrop.airdrop function uses _amounts.length as the loop bound but reads from both _amounts.offset and _recipients.offset in raw calldata. If _recipients.length < _amounts.length, the loop reads past the recipients array into adjacent calldata, sending tokens to garbage addresses derived from other calldata fields. Additionally, sum(amounts) is never compared to _total, allowing excess tokens to be permanently trapped in the contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: Assembly-based functions bypass Solidity's built-in safety checks. The lack of length validation and total reconciliation are classic assembly pitfalls that can lead to fund loss.

proof_of_signal.pattern: let sz := _amounts.length used as loop bound; _recipients not validated; calldataload reads past recipients array

preconditions: Caller provides mismatched array lengths (_recipients shorter than _amounts)
Or caller provides _total > sum(_amounts)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Mismatched arrays send tokens to garbage addresses; excess _total tokens stuck permanently
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Array length comparison and total reconciliation

analysis_questions: What calldata values get interpreted as addresses when reading past _recipients?
Can the stuck tokens be recovered?
Is there any caller-side validation before calling airdrop?
validation_requirements: Call airdrop with _recipients.length < _amounts.length, verify garbage address transfers
Call airdrop with _total > sum(_amounts), verify excess stuck in contract

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A misconfigured off-chain system calls airdrop with mismatched arrays. Tokens are sent to unintended addresses derived from adjacent calldata, resulting in irreversible fund loss.
prerequisites: Caller makes an error in array construction

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent loss of airdropped tokens to garbage addresses or contract
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Intended airdrop recipients who don't receive tokens
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None - tokens at garbage addresses are unrecoverable

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for mismatched array lengths

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable post-hoc by comparing events to intended recipients

threats.title: Bonding.buy() and sell() Missing nonReentrant: State Updates After External Calls
threats.hypothesis_description: Bonding.buy() and sell() lack nonReentrant modifiers. They read pair reserves, execute router calls (which involve token transfers), and then update tokenInfo state afterwards. If the asset token has transfer hooks (ERC777-compatible) or if any intermediate call allows re-entrance to Bonding, the tokenInfo state can be read in a stale state during re-entry. Critically, the graduation check in buy() occurs AFTER the router call but BEFORE tokenInfo is updated.
threats.investigation_rationale: The checks-effects-interactions pattern is violated: external calls via router happen before tokenInfo state updates. While the router itself has nonReentrant, re-entry into Bonding.buy/sell/unwrapToken from a token callback is not prevented.
deployment_assumptions: Asset token must have transfer hooks or callbacks for reentrancy to be exploitable
threats.config_evidence: Asset token is VIRTUAL (standard ERC20 without hooks per Virtual.sol). Unless a different token is configured, reentrancy via token callbacks is unlikely with standard ERC20.

proof_of_signal.pattern: router.buy() external call at line 178; tokenInfo state updates at lines 181-197; no nonReentrant modifier on buy()

preconditions: Asset token or traded token has transfer callback mechanism
Callback allows re-entry into Bonding functions

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: External calls via router before tokenInfo state updates; no nonReentrant
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Re-entry reads stale tokenInfo; graduation threshold check uses stale reserves
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: nonReentrant modifier on buy() and sell()

analysis_questions: What token types can be traded on the bonding curve?
Does the FERC20 token have any callback mechanism?
Can re-entry trigger double graduation or stale state reads?
validation_requirements: Deploy with ERC777-compatible asset token, test reentrancy on buy/sell
Verify tokenInfo state consistency during re-entrant calls

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If a token with transfer hooks is used, an attacker triggers a callback during router.buy() that re-enters Bonding.sell() on the same token. Sell reads stale tokenInfo (pre-buy), potentially getting incorrect price calculations.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Stale state manipulation; potential incorrect graduation parameters
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All holders of the affected token

threats.test_coverage_gap: No reentrancy test for buy/sell in Bonding

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring unusual call patterns

threats.title: Bonding.unwrapToken allows anyone to force-convert arbitrary holders by burning their FERC20 and pulling agent tokens from pair
threats.hypothesis_description: unwrapToken() is publicly callable with an arbitrary list of accounts. It burns each account's FERC20 balance via burnFrom() (Bonding is token owner) and then transfers agent tokens from the pair to that account. This enables forced conversion/griefing at scale and may break user expectations/strategies, and can be weaponized to trigger adverse tax/accounting/locking consequences in downstream systems.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is explicitly identified by Phase A as an access invariant violation. It is a concrete, on-chain, unprivileged call path that affects arbitrary users.

proof_of_signal.pattern: public unwrapToken() loops accounts; calls token.burnFrom(acc,balance) then agentToken.transferFrom(pairAddress, acc, balance) with no access control / consent check

preconditions: tokenInfo[srcTokenAddress].tradingOnUniswap == true (graduated)
Bonding is the owner of the deployed FERC20 token (true if token created via new FERC20 inside Bonding)
PairAddress holds agentToken balance and has approved transfers or transferFrom is otherwise possible (depends on router.approval step during graduation)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: unrestricted third-party can burn others' balances via owner privilege
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Anyone can force conversion for any holder list (griefing/forced asset migration)
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No require(msg.sender==acc) and no role/owner gate on unwrapToken

analysis_questions: Does agentToken.transferFrom(pairAddress, acc, balance) always succeed (approval set correctly) and is the allowance unlimited?
Can this be used to indirectly drain agent tokens or only force-transfer to the same account being burned?
What downstream integrations assume FERC20 remains held (staking, governance, vesting, tax)?
validation_requirements: Test unwrapToken called by an arbitrary attacker for third-party holders post-graduation
Confirm allowance/approval path from router.approval(pairAddress, agentToken, address(this), ...)
Assess griefing impact and whether any value can be extracted via induced state changes

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: After graduation, attacker scrapes holder addresses and calls unwrapToken(token, holders[]) to forcibly burn their FERC20 and move agent tokens to them, potentially breaking user positions and causing downstream losses/DoS.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Griefing/forced conversion; potential secondary losses if agent token differs in transferability/tax or if integrations rely on FERC20
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All holders of graduated bonding tokens
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Unclear; conversion is irreversible due to burn

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Yes (large holder lists may hit block gas limits; attacker can still target subsets)
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitor burn Transfer events + agentToken Transfer events

threats.title: FRouter.graduate can be abused by any EXECUTOR_ROLE to drain all assetToken from a pair regardless of token state
threats.hypothesis_description: graduate(tokenAddress) transfers the entire assetToken balance from the pair to msg.sender (the executor), with no checks that graduation conditions are met (e.g., token reached threshold, trading disabled, one-time graduation). Any address holding EXECUTOR_ROLE can pull liquidity at any time, potentially breaking the bonding market and diverting assets.
threats.investigation_rationale: While role-gated, EXECUTOR_ROLE is often assigned to operational hot wallets/contracts (e.g., Bonding). If compromised or mis-assigned, this becomes an immediate drain. Even without compromise, this is a sharp edge: no invariant enforcement in code.
deployment_assumptions: EXECUTOR_ROLE is assigned to at least one externally reachable contract or operational EOA beyond strictly trusted automation
Pair holds non-trivial assetToken balance (e.g., from trading fees or liquidity)
threats.config_evidence: No scripts shown for FRouter role assignments (ADMIN_ROLE/EXECUTOR_ROLE) beyond initialize granting DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE only; role provisioning is external/missing in deployment context.

proof_of_signal.pattern: graduate() transfers full assetBalance from pair to msg.sender without checking tokenInfo state or one-time guard

preconditions: Caller has EXECUTOR_ROLE on FRouter
Pair exists for (tokenAddress, assetToken)
Pair has assetToken balance > 0

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: privileged drain without state machine guard
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Liquidity extraction can occur even if token not meant to graduate yet
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No check of graduation threshold, no 'already graduated' flag, no recipient restriction

analysis_questions: Who receives EXECUTOR_ROLE in production (Bonding, multisig, automation)?
Is graduate intended to only be callable from Bonding._openTradingOnUniswap (and is that enforced anywhere)?
Can an executor repeatedly call graduate to drain newly accumulated asset balances (fees)?
validation_requirements: Review deployment/role assignment transactions for FRouter
Simulate calling graduate pre-threshold and observe pool impact
Check whether FPair/router interactions allow repeated extraction

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If attacker obtains EXECUTOR_ROLE (misconfig/compromise), they call graduate(token) to pull all asset tokens from the pair to themselves, collapsing the market and stealing assets.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Drain of all assetToken liquidity from targeted pairs; market collapse
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All traders/holders of tokens in the bonding system
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Low; requires admin intervention and recapitalization

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests ensuring graduate is only callable in correct state

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Track Transfer events from pair tokenB to executor

threats.title: FPair.mint initializes k = reserve0*reserve1 without overflow protection; extreme params could brick pair or misprice
threats.hypothesis_description: FPair.mint computes k as reserve0 * reserve1 in checked arithmetic. If either reserve is large enough to overflow uint256, mint will revert and permanently block pool initialization. If reserves are attacker-influenced via EXECUTOR_ROLE paths (router.addInitialLiquidity), malicious parameters could intentionally DoS new token launches.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a correctness issue in a critical AMM primitive. While typical reserve sizes won't overflow, the protocol uses virtual liquidity calculations and could pass large values if misconfigured or if a bug leads to huge reserves.
deployment_assumptions: An attacker or misconfigured executor can cause router.addInitialLiquidity to call mint with very large reserve values (near 2^256)
No upstream bounds exist on initialSupply/liquidity computations
threats.config_evidence: No explicit bounds on initialSupply or liquidity in Bonding.launchFor; liquidity formula involves multiple 1e18 scalings and could become large if parameters are mis-set by owner.

proof_of_signal.pattern: k: reserve0 * reserve1 computed directly in checked arithmetic; no bounding/safe mul

preconditions: A call path exists that supplies extreme reserve0/reserve1 into mint() (via router.addInitialLiquidity)
Pair has not already been minted (_pool.lastUpdated == 0)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: unchecked parameter magnitude for multiplication in state initialization
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: overflow revert can brick new pools; mispricing if upstream arithmetic saturates/rounds
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No max bounds on reserves, no use of mulDiv or 512-bit mul

analysis_questions: What are realistic upper bounds for supply/liquidity from Bonding.launchFor given initialSupply, assetRate, K scaling?
Can owner-controlled params create a reserve overflow intentionally or accidentally?
Would a reverted mint block future recovery (can pair be recreated or does mapping lock it)?
validation_requirements: Fuzz Bonding.launchFor liquidity computation to determine reachable magnitudes
Test mint overflow and observe whether factory can create a new pair or remains stuck

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker (or malicious owner) sets parameters so that launchFor computes huge liquidity/reserve values; mint overflows and reverts, preventing token launch and causing DoS on platform activity.
prerequisites: Control over parameters or access to launch path with extreme inputs

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Primarily DoS of launches; potential loss of fees/opportunity
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Creators attempting to launch tokens; platform availability
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Moderate if admin can change params or redeploy

threats.test_coverage_gap: No fuzz/property tests for reserve magnitude bounds

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitor mint reverts and PairCreated without subsequent Mint events

threats.title: TBABonus.updateBonusRate emits swapped old/new values, potentially breaking off-chain accounting and bonus governance controls
threats.hypothesis_description: updateBonusRate stores oldBonusRate then sets bonusRate, but emits BonusRateUpdated(bonusRate_, oldBonusRate) (new, old) while the event signature suggests (old, new). Off-chain systems consuming this event could invert rate changes, miscomputing payouts and monitoring.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete code defect in an admin-controlled parameter update with direct financial implications (bonuses). Even though it doesn't change on-chain behavior, monitoring and accounting systems often rely on events.

proof_of_signal.pattern: emit BonusRateUpdated(bonusRate_, oldBonusRate); parameters appear reversed vs event name (old,new)

preconditions: Off-chain services (indexers, analytics, accounting) rely on BonusRateUpdated(old,new) ordering
ADMIN_ROLE uses updateBonusRate in production

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: monitoring/accounting may record inverted rate transitions
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No tests asserting event parameters match state transition

analysis_questions: Do any protocol services compute bonuses from events rather than reading state directly?
Is BonusRateUpdated documented/consumed externally with assumed (old,new) ordering?
Are there similar event-order bugs elsewhere?
validation_requirements: Unit test: call updateBonusRate and compare emitted values vs stored state
Review off-chain code consuming BonusRateUpdated

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attackers cannot directly change the rate, but can exploit monitoring confusion (e.g., to mask malicious admin changes) if observers misinterpret the event as decreasing when it actually increases.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Indirect; mis-accounting of bonus payouts or delayed detection of harmful parameter changes
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Creators/recipients expecting correct bonus rate tracking
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: High (fix event or ensure consumers read state)

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Compare on-chain bonusRate state vs event stream

threats.title: FERC20 burnFrom Does Not Decrement totalSupply Creating Supply Mismatch at Graduation
threats.hypothesis_description: FERC20.burnFrom and _burn reduce _balances[user] but do NOT decrement _totalSupply. During graduation, _openTradingOnUniswap passes _token.data.supply (original totalSupply) and tokenBalance to executeBondingCurveApplication. If the factory uses these to determine agent token allocation, the agent token supply is based on inflated FERC20 supply. Additionally, unwrapToken uses a 1:1 FERC20-to-agent-token mapping, but the pair may only hold lpSupply worth of agent tokens (tokenBalance at graduation), while users collectively hold S-tokenBalance FERC20 tokens. If more than half the supply was bought by users, there are insufficient agent tokens in the pair for all holders to unwrap.
threats.investigation_rationale: The burnFrom function explicitly reduces balance without reducing totalSupply, violating the ERC20 conservation invariant. This has downstream effects on graduation economics where supply parameters are derived from stale data.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _balances[user] -= amount without _totalSupply -= amount in both _burn and burnFrom

preconditions: Token graduates through bonding curve (gradThreshold reached)
Users hold more than half of FERC20 supply at graduation
AgentFactoryV3 allocates lpSupply tokens to vault and remainder elsewhere

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: _balances[user] -= amount but _totalSupply unchanged; totalSupply() returns inflated value

threats.title: Bonding.launch() Permanently Broken Due to Double NonReentrant Guard
threats.hypothesis_description: Bonding.launch() calls launchFor() internally, but both functions carry the nonReentrant modifier from ReentrancyGuardUpgradeable. threats.title: Bonding.setFee Missing Ether Scaling Creates Fee Miscalculation
threats.hypothesis_description: In Bonding.initialize(), fee is calculated as `(fee_ * 1 ether) / 1000`, applying ether scaling. However, setFee() directly assigns `fee = newFee` without the same scaling. If the owner calls setFee intending the same semantics as initialization (e.g., passing 5 to mean 0.5%), the fee becomes 5 wei instead of 5e15 wei. This makes the fee requirement `purchaseAmount > fee` trivially satisfied, collecting essentially zero fees. Conversely, if the owner passes an already-scaled value, the comparison works but there's no guard or documentation enforcing this.
threats.investigation_rationale: The asymmetric scaling between initialize and setFee is a code defect. The setter doesn't apply the same transformation as the initializer, creating a maintenance trap that can lead to zero or incorrect fees.

threats.title: Anyone can grief AeroAdaptor users by draining their approved tokenIn via swapExactTokensForTokens where outputs are sent to attacker-chosen `to`
threats.hypothesis_description: AeroAdaptor.swapExactTokensForTokens transfers `tokenIn` from `msg.sender` to the adaptor, then calls the underlying Aerodrome router with `to` provided by the caller. If a user (or contract) mistakenly grants allowance to AeroAdaptor, any third party cannot directly steal their funds; however, if the user is a contract that exposes a call path to AeroAdaptor (or uses a shared executor), an attacker can trigger swaps that send outputs to attacker-controlled `to` while pulling tokenIn from that contract. This is especially relevant for automation systems that hold allowances to router adaptors.
threats.investigation_rationale: Adaptor contracts are often approved by treasuries/bots. The adaptor has no access control and does not enforce `to == msg.sender`. In operational environments, a compromised strategy/executor that can call the adaptor can redirect swap proceeds without needing to move allowances elsewhere.

threats.title: promptMulti caches agentTba incorrectly when agentIds repeat non-contiguously, potentially sending payments to the wrong agent TBA
threats.hypothesis_description: AgentInference.promptMulti() attempts to cache the TBA lookup by tracking `prevAgentId`, but it never updates `prevAgentId` inside the loop. As a result, if the first element sets `agentTba` once, subsequent iterations will treat every agentId as different (since prevAgentId stays 0 unless agentId==0), repeatedly overwriting agentTba; more importantly, the intended caching optimization is broken, and the code’s correctness depends on always updating agentTba for each iteration. This is a code smell that warrants verifying that payments always go to the correct TBA for each agentId and that no edge cases (e.g., agentId==0) can route funds to address(0) or a stale TBA.
threats.investigation_rationale: Inference payments are direct token transfers to TBAs. Any mismatch between agentId and recipient TBA causes mispayment (funds loss and accounting disputes). Even if the current logic happens to work (by reloading often), the broken caching indicates insufficient test coverage and raises the risk of subtle routing bugs during upgrades/refactors.

agentNft.virtualInfo(agentId).tba returns non-zero for valid agentIds

threats.title: Genesis.onGenesisFailed can be permanently DoS’d by sparse index lists due to refundUserCountForFailed needing to equal participants.length
threats.hypothesis_description: Genesis.onGenesisFailed processes refunds only for the provided participantIndexes, incrementing refundUserCountForFailed once per refunded participant. It marks isFailed only when refundUserCountForFailed == participants.length. If the operator repeatedly calls onGenesisFailed with index sets that omit some participants (or if some participants have zero virtuals due to prior refunds/logic), isFailed may never be reached, leaving genesis in a non-terminal ended-but-not-failed state. This can block flows that require whenFinalized or isFailed for administrative cleanup and may confuse refund expectations.
threats.investigation_rationale: While only FACTORY_ROLE can call onGenesisFailed, this is still a correctness and liveness risk: the failure finalization depends on a counter reaching an exact value rather than tracking unique refunds or iterating over all participants deterministically. Validate whether this can brick finalization in realistic operational scenarios (large participant sets, partial batching, repeated calls).

threats.title: Truncation in FRouter constant-product math allows draining pair reserves
threats.hypothesis_description: FRouter.getAmountsOut calculates the new reserve using integer division (k / newReserve). Because integer division truncates (rounds down), the calculated newReserve is smaller than the exact mathematical value. The output amount is calculated as (reserve - newReserve), meaning the rounding down of the reserve results in rounding UP the amountOut. The user receives more tokens than the constant product curve dictates. FPair.swap does not enforce the k-invariant (reserve0 * reserve1 >= k), so the actual product of the pool's reserves strictly decreases with each swap. An attacker can perform many small swaps optimized to maximize the fractional truncation, systematically draining the pool's assets.
threats.investigation_rationale: Custom AMM math is highly susceptible to rounding errors. The lack of a k-invariant check in the pair contract means rounding errors accumulate in the attacker's favor, breaking the fundamental economic security of the bonding curve.

proof_of_signal.pattern: newReserveA = k / newReserveB; amountOut = reserveA - newReserveA;

preconditions: FPair has liquidity
Attacker can perform multiple swaps in a loop

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: amountOut = reserveA - (k / newReserveB)
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Integer division truncation increases amountOut artificially
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Repeated swaps extract more value than deposited, draining the pool reserves over time

threats.title: FRouter.buy Tax Computation Uses Division by 100 With Unbounded Tax Rate - Potential DoS or Value Extraction
threats.hypothesis_description: FRouter.buy() calculates fees as `txFee = (fee * amountIn) / 100` where `fee = factory.buyTax()`. FFactory.setTaxParams() has NO upper bound validation on buyTax_ or sellTax_. If buyTax >= 100, then txFee >= amountIn, causing `amount = amountIn - txFee` to underflow (revert in Solidity 0.8). This permanently DoSes all buy operations on the bonding curve. Similarly for sellTax >= 100 in sell(). Values between 50-99 extract 50-99% of user funds as fees.
threats.investigation_rationale: The tax rate has no bounds checking and directly controls the fee extraction from all bonding curve trades. A misconfiguration or malicious admin action could either DoS all trading or extract excessive fees from users.

threats.title: FPair.mint With Zero Reserve Creates k=0 Enabling Free Token Extraction
threats.hypothesis_description: FPair.mint() sets k = reserve0 * reserve1 with no validation that either value is non-zero. If either reserve is 0, k = 0. With k=0, FRouter.getAmountsOut calculates newReserveA = k / newReserveB = 0, so amountOut = reserveA - 0 = reserveA (the entire token reserve). This means anyone could buy ALL tokens in the pair for any input amount.
threats.investigation_rationale: A zero-k pair would break the constant-product AMM invariant entirely, allowing the entire token supply to be extracted in a single transaction.

threats.title: Genesis.onGenesisSuccess Repeatable Invocation Overwrites Unclaimed Token Distributions
threats.hypothesis_description: Genesis.onGenesisSuccess lacks a terminal success state flag. After the first successful call (agentTokenAddress set, claimableAgentTokens populated), the function can be called again by FACTORY_ROLE. The second call passes all modifiers (whenNotCancelled, whenNotFailed, whenEnded) and the refundUserCountForFailed==0 check. Since claimableAgentTokens uses `=` assignment (not `+=`), the second call overwrites unclaimed allocations with new values. OPERATION_ROLE can redirect agent tokens from legitimate users to attacker-controlled addresses.
threats.investigation_rationale: The absence of a success-terminal guard combined with overwrite (not accumulate) semantics creates a code-level vulnerability. While exploitation requires OPERATION_ROLE, this is a code bug — the function should not be callable twice.

threats.title: BondingTax.updateSwapParams lacks slippage bounds, allowing slippage > 100% and minOutput underflow-style behavior (minOutput=0) harming treasury swaps
threats.hypothesis_description: BondingTax.updateSwapParams sets `_slippage` without bounding it to <= 10000. In swapForAsset, minOutput = expectedOutput * (10000 - _slippage) / 10000. If _slippage > 10000, (10000 - _slippage) underflows and reverts in Solidity 0.8, causing swapForAsset to revert permanently; if _slippage is set to exactly 10000, minOutput becomes 0, removing slippage protection and allowing extremely bad execution prices.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a configuration-sensitive but code-level missing validation on a safety-critical parameter. Even with a trusted admin, mistakes happen; if admin key compromised (out of scope for HIGH realism), attacker can set catastrophic values. Validate whether the system has guardrails elsewhere.

threats.title: AeroAdaptor ignores the user-supplied `path` and always swaps tokenIn->tokenOut, enabling token routing confusion and stuck funds in adaptor
threats.hypothesis_description: AeroAdaptor.swapExactTokensForTokens accepts a `path` parameter but ignores it, always constructing a single Aerodrome route using stored tokenIn/tokenOut/factory. If integrated as a generic IRouter, callers may believe they are swapping along the provided path, but the adaptor will attempt a different swap. Additionally, the adaptor pulls tokenIn from msg.sender into itself but never forwards tokenIn to the Aerodrome router explicitly; it relies on the Aerodrome router pulling from the adaptor via allowance. This can lead to stuck balances if swaps revert after transferFrom or if tokenIn/tokenOut mismatch the caller's intent.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is an integration-footgun that can cause loss of funds by misrouting swaps or sticking tokenIn in the adaptor contract. While attackers can’t generally steal others’ funds via this alone, real users/protocol components can be harmed if this is used as a drop-in UniswapV2 router.
deployment_assumptions: AeroAdaptor is wired as the router for AgentTax/BondingTax (router_ points to adaptor) or other modules that expect `path` to be respected
tokenIn/tokenOut stored in adaptor do not match the module’s expected taxToken/assetToken pair

threats.title: LPRefund.manualRefund Lacks Txhash Uniqueness Check - Admin Can Issue Double Refunds
threats.hypothesis_description: LPRefund.manualRefund uses `refunds[txhash] += amount` instead of checking if the txhash was already used. Unlike the regular refund() function which reverts on duplicate txhashes, manualRefund can be called multiple times with the same txhash, each time transferring additional taxToken to the recipient. This allows ADMIN_ROLE to drain the entire taxToken balance by repeatedly refunding the same transaction.
threats.investigation_rationale: The asymmetry between refund() (which has replay protection) and manualRefund() (which doesn't) suggests a code oversight. The += operator accumulates amounts without any upper bound check, enabling fund extraction limited only by contract balance.

threats.title: FFactory initialization omits ADMIN_ROLE assignment, risking permanent inability to set router/tax params (dead-end ops state)
threats.hypothesis_description: FFactory.initialize() grants only DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE to msg.sender, but does not grant ADMIN_ROLE or CREATOR_ROLE. Critical functions setRouter() and setTaxParams() require onlyRole(ADMIN_ROLE), and createPair requires CREATOR_ROLE. If deployment scripts or post-deploy ops do not explicitly grant these roles, the factory can be stuck unable to configure router or create pairs, bricking the AMM subsystem.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a common operational deployment gap with real production impact. Validate role provisioning in deployment and upgrades; if missing, protocol may become non-functional or require redeploy.
deployment_assumptions: No post-deploy transaction grants ADMIN_ROLE and CREATOR_ROLE to intended operators
DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE holder does not execute role grants (or is renounced/lost)
threats.config_evidence: No deployment script for FFactory included that grants ADMIN_ROLE/CREATOR_ROLE; code only grants DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE in initialize() (contracts/fun/FFactory.sol).

proof_of_signal.pattern: initialize(): _grantRole(DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE, msg.sender); no _grantRole(ADMIN_ROLE/CREATOR_ROLE, ...)

preconditions: FFactory is deployed as upgradeable proxy or directly and initialize is called
Operators assume deployer automatically has ADMIN_ROLE/CREATOR_ROLE (but it does not)
No later grantRole calls are made

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Role-gated critical setters exist but roles are not assigned during init.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Deployment/ops must grant roles correctly; otherwise dead-end.

operational_questions: Where are ADMIN_ROLE and CREATOR_ROLE granted in production?
Is there a runbook ensuring role setup after initialization?
Is DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE retained by a multisig or renounced?
validation_requirements: Review on-chain role members for FFactory on target networks
Review deployment scripts for role-grant transactions (none provided here)

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Not attacker-driven; manifests as an ops misconfiguration leading to outage.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: If attacker can influence deployment parameters (unlikely), encourage misconfigured deployment causing non-functional factory.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Cannot set router or create pairs; AMM subsystem unusable.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium—requires privileged role recovery or redeploy.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: ADMIN_ROLE has zero members; CREATOR_ROLE has zero members
